section.title: The New Life in Christ
section.sectionRationale: Having called the church to walk worthy and grow into maturity, Paul now spells out what that new life looks like in practice. He contrasts the darkened, futile way of the Gentiles with the way believers came to know Christ, then frames the whole Christian life as a great exchange: put off the old self and put on the new, created to be like God. From that principle he draws a string of concrete commands about truth, anger, work, and speech, all shaped by love for one's neighbor and grief at grieving the Spirit. The chapter ends where the gospel begins, with kindness and forgiveness grounded in how God has forgiven us in Christ.
section.thesis: Because they have learned Christ, believers must no longer walk in the futility and corruption of the old self but put it off and put on the new self, created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness, living out that renewal in truthful speech, righteous anger, honest work, gracious words, and tenderhearted forgiveness that mirrors God's forgiveness of them in Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: no longer walking as the Gentiles in futility and darkness; the great exchange of putting off the old self and putting on the new; the new life worked out in truth, anger, work, and speech; and kindness and forgiveness after the pattern of God in Christ.

units.label: No longer walk as the Gentiles

verses.text: So I tell you this, and insist on it in the Lord, that you must no longer walk as the Gentiles do, in the {{mataiotes|futility}} of their thinking.
They are darkened in their understanding and alienated from the life of God because of the ignorance that is in them due to the hardness of their hearts.
Having lost all sense of shame, they have given themselves over to sensuality for the practice of every kind of impurity, with a craving for more.

units.label: Put off the old self, put on the new

verses.text: But this is not the way you came to know Christ.
Surely you heard of Him and were taught in Him, in keeping with the truth that is in Jesus,
to {{apotithemi|put off}} your former way of life, your old self, which is being corrupted by its deceitful desires;
to be {{ananeoo|renewed}} in the spirit of your minds;
and to {{enduo|put on}} the {{kainos|new}} self, created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness.

units.label: The new life in practice: truth, anger, work, speech

verses.text: Therefore each of you must put off falsehood and speak truthfully to his neighbor, for we are all members of one another.
"Be angry, yet do not sin." Do not let the sun set upon your anger,
and do not give the devil a foothold.
He who has been stealing must steal no longer, but must work, doing good with his own hands, that he may have something to share with the one in need.
Let no unwholesome talk come out of your mouths, but only what is helpful for building up the one in need and bringing grace to those who listen.
And do not {{lupeo|grieve}} the Holy Spirit of God, in whom you were sealed for the day of redemption.

units.label: Kindness and forgiveness
units.note: as God in Christ forgave you

verses.text: Get rid of all bitterness, rage and anger, outcry and slander, along with every form of malice.
Be kind and tenderhearted to one another, {{aphiemi|forgiving}} each other just as in Christ God forgave you.

mataiotes.gloss: Futility, emptiness, vanity, purposelessness. It is the word the Greek Old Testament uses for the vanity of idols and the fleeting emptiness of life apart from God. Paul says the Gentile mind runs in futility, thinking that leads nowhere because it is cut off from the life of God. The renewed mind, by contrast, is filled with the truth that is in Jesus.

apotithemi.gloss: To put off, lay aside, or strip away, as one removes and discards soiled clothing. Paul uses it for casting off the old self with its deceitful desires and for putting away falsehood. The image is of decisively taking off the garment of the former life so that it is left behind, not merely loosened while still worn.

ananeoo.gloss: To be renewed, made new again, refreshed in the inner life. It describes an ongoing renewal in the spirit of the mind, the deep reorientation of thought and desire that the Spirit works in believers. The old futile thinking is replaced as the mind is continually made new, so that the whole person is reshaped toward God.

enduo.gloss: To put on, clothe oneself with, as one dresses in a garment. Paired with putting off, it completes the picture of the great exchange: the old self is stripped away and the new self is put on. Believers are to clothe themselves with the new humanity, created to be like God, wearing a life that answers to their new identity in Christ.

kainos.gloss: New, fresh, of a new kind and quality, not merely new in time but new in nature. The new self is not a patched version of the old but a fresh creation, made to be like God in true righteousness and holiness. The word points to the newness of the new creation begun in Christ, a humanity remade after the image of its Maker.

lupeo.gloss: To grieve, cause sorrow or pain. Strikingly, Paul says sin grieves the Holy Spirit of God, treating the Spirit as a person who can be pained by the sin of those He indwells. The word reveals both the personhood of the Spirit and the closeness of His relationship to believers, in whom He dwells and by whom they are sealed for the day of redemption.

aphiemi.gloss: To forgive, release, let go, send away, cancel a debt. The same verb runs through the New Testament for God's forgiveness of sins. Here believers are to forgive one another with the very forgiveness they have received, letting go of wrongs as God in Christ has let go of theirs. Forgiveness given flows from forgiveness received.

basic.context: Paul now shows what the new life looks like in daily practice. He insists that believers must no longer live like the surrounding pagans, whose minds run in futility, whose understanding is darkened, and who have given themselves over to impurity. That is not how they learned Christ. The heart of the Christian life is a great exchange: put off the old self, which is rotting under its deceitful desires, be renewed in mind, and put on the new self, created to be like God. From there flow concrete commands, speak truth, deal with anger before it festers, work honestly and generously, use words that build others up, and do not grieve the Spirit. The chapter closes by rooting kindness and forgiveness in the forgiveness God has shown in Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage moves from contrast to principle to practice. Verses 17 to 19 describe the old pagan way, verses 20 to 24 give the governing principle of putting off and putting on, and verses 25 to 32 apply it to specific areas of life. Each command is grounded in a gospel reason: we are members of one another, we are sealed by the Spirit, we have been forgiven in Christ. The imperatives are not bare rules but the working out of the new identity believers already have.
basic.originalLanguages: The Gentile mind runs in mataiotes, futility, an empty purposelessness cut off from God. Believers are to apotithemi, put off, the old self like soiled clothing, to be ananeoo, renewed, in the spirit of the mind, and to enduo, put on, the new self, which is kainos, new in kind, a fresh creation like God. Sin is serious enough to lupeo, grieve, the Holy Spirit who seals us. And the summons is to aphiemi, forgive, one another as God in Christ has forgiven us.
basic.typology: The new self, created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness, echoes the first creation of humanity in the image of God, now restored and remade in Christ. Where Adam fell into futility and darkness, the new humanity in Christ is renewed after the likeness of its Maker, a new creation putting on what was lost in the fall.
basic.christ: The whole passage turns on knowing Christ. Believers did not learn futility and impurity; they learned Christ, were taught in Him, according to the truth that is in Jesus. The new self is put on in union with Him, and the climax grounds all forgiveness in what God has done in Christ, forgiving us so fully that we can forgive one another. Christ is both the pattern and the power of the new life.
basic.god: God is the pattern of the new humanity, which is created to be like Him in true righteousness and holiness. His Spirit indwells believers and can be grieved by their sin, showing how personal and near He is. And His forgiveness in Christ is the fountain from which all human forgiveness flows. The God who made us in His image now remakes us to be like Him and forgives us freely.
basic.crossReferences: Colossians 3 gives the same picture of putting off the old self and putting on the new, created after the image of its Maker. Romans 12 calls for the renewing of the mind. Romans 6 speaks of walking in newness of life after dying to sin with Christ.
basic.oneStory: This is the renewal of the image of God in fallen humanity. Where sin darkened the mind and corrupted the heart, God is remaking a people to be like Him again, undoing the futility that followed the fall. The new self put on here is the redeemed humanity the whole story has been moving toward, a people forgiven and made new, reflecting the God who forgave them in Christ.

context.literary: The passage falls into a clear pattern. Verses 17 to 19 describe the old Gentile walk in the language of futility, darkness, and impurity. Verses 20 to 24 turn to Christ with a sharp but this is not how you learned Christ, and set out the governing metaphor of putting off, being renewed, and putting on. Verses 25 to 32 apply the principle in a series of therefore commands, each pairing a negative to reject with a positive to embrace, and closing with the great motive of God's forgiveness in Christ.
context.historical: Ephesus was a center of pagan religion, magic, and sexual license, so Paul's description of Gentile life would have named the very world his readers had come out of. Many in the church had once lived in the futility and impurity he describes. The call to put off the old self was not abstract but a summons to leave behind a whole culture of darkness and to live as a distinct new humanity.
context.canonical: This passage stands alongside Colossians 3:1-17 as the New Testament's fullest teaching on the put off and put on pattern of sanctification. Its vision of the new self created after the image of God ties back to Genesis and forward to the ethical teaching of the whole New Testament, grounding practical holiness in the believer's new identity in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press the church to abandon the futile, darkened way of life it once shared with the pagan world and to live out the new identity it has in Christ, putting off the old self and putting on the new, so that renewed minds produce truthful, generous, gracious, and forgiving lives.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 17 to 19 describe the old way of life as a warning, not a model. Verses 22 to 24 describe the exchange that has taken place in Christ, which then grounds the prescriptions that follow. The commands of verses 25 to 32 are prescription, but they flow from the indicative of who believers now are, the new self already created to be like God.

debates.question: How should we read Be angry, yet do not sin in verse 26?

views.label: Permission for righteous anger, tightly limited
views.case: Some read it as allowing a righteous anger against sin and injustice, while sharply limiting it: do not let it become sinful, and do not let it linger past sundown or give the devil an opening.

views.label: A concessive warning, if you are angry
views.case: Others read it as conceding that anger will arise, while urging believers not to sin in it: even when anger comes, deal with it quickly and do not let it fester into bitterness or revenge.

debates.bearing: On either reading the aim is the same: anger must not be allowed to take root or turn to sin; it is to be dealt with quickly before it gives the devil a foothold, and it must not harden into the bitterness and malice of verse 31.

study.originalLanguages: Paul contrasts two ways of life through vivid words. The Gentile mind is trapped in mataiotes, futility, an empty and aimless thinking severed from the life of God. Against this, the believer is called to a great change of clothing: to apotithemi, put off, the old self like a filthy garment corrupted by deceitful desires; to be ananeoo, renewed, an ongoing renewal in the very spirit of the mind; and to enduo, put on, the new self as a fresh garment. That new self is kainos, new in kind and quality, a fresh creation made to be like God in true righteousness and holiness. The seriousness of sin appears when Paul says it can lupeo, grieve, the Holy Spirit, treating the Spirit as a person who feels the pain of the sin of those He seals. And the passage closes with aphiemi, to forgive, the releasing of wrongs that believers extend to one another because God in Christ has first released theirs.

typology.type: The new self created to be like God
typology.fulfillment: Restores and fulfills humanity made in the image of God in Genesis, now remade in true righteousness and holiness in Christ.

typology.type: Putting off the old and putting on the new
typology.fulfillment: Enacts the death and resurrection of Christ in the believer's life, dying to the old humanity of Adam and rising into the new humanity of Christ.

typology.type: Forgiveness after the pattern of God in Christ
typology.fulfillment: The forgiveness accomplished at the cross becomes the model and motive for the forgiveness believers extend to one another.

study.christ: Christ is the center of the new life. Believers came to know Him, were taught in Him, according to the truth that is in Jesus, and this knowledge is what breaks them from the old pagan way. The new self is put on in union with Him, the new humanity created after God's likeness. Most movingly, the whole ethic of the passage is anchored in Him at the end: we forgive one another because in Christ God forgave us. His cross is both the ground of our forgiveness and the pattern for ours. To learn Christ is to leave the old life and be remade in Him.
study.god: This passage reveals God as holy, personal, and forgiving. The new self is created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness, so His own character is the standard of the new humanity. His Spirit dwells so intimately in believers that their sin can grieve Him, and by that Spirit they are sealed for the day of redemption. And His forgiveness in Christ is so complete that it becomes the measure of how they must forgive one another. The God who made humanity in His image now remakes it and forgives it freely.

crossReferences.note: The same put off and put on teaching, with the new self renewed in knowledge after the image of its Creator, closely parallel to verses 22 to 24.
Be transformed by the renewing of your mind, echoing the renewal in the spirit of the mind in verse 23.
The old self crucified with Christ so that we walk in newness of life, underlying the exchange of old for new here.
Forgive others as you have been forgiven, matching the motive for forgiveness in verse 32.

study.oneStory: This passage carries forward the Bible's story of the image of God restored. Sin darkened the mind and corrupted the heart, plunging humanity into the futility Paul describes, but in Christ God is remaking a people to be like Him once more, in true righteousness and holiness. The old self of Adam is put off and the new self of Christ is put on, a new creation begun now and completed at the day of redemption for which the Spirit has sealed us. The forgiven people who forgive one another are a living sign of the reconciled humanity toward which the whole story moves.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the way of life he tells believers to abandon?

options.text: Futile in thinking, darkened in understanding, and given over to impurity.
options.note: Verses 17 to 19 describe the futile, darkened, sensual way of the Gentiles.

options.text: Wise and enlightened but simply outdated.
options.note: Paul calls it futile and darkened, not wise; it is alienated from the life of God.

options.text: Harmless and morally neutral.
options.note: It is given over to impurity with a craving for more, not neutral.

options.text: The way believers should continue to live.
options.note: Paul insists they must no longer walk this way.

quiz.q: What is the great exchange Paul describes in verses 22 to 24?

options.text: Put off the old self and put on the new self, created to be like God.
options.note: The heart of the passage is stripping off the old and clothing oneself in the new.

options.text: Trade one set of rules for another with no inner change.
options.note: The exchange includes being renewed in the spirit of the mind, not just new rules.

options.text: Improve the old self gradually while keeping it on.
options.note: The old self is to be put off, not merely improved and kept.

options.text: Wait passively for God to change everything without any response.
options.note: Believers are called to put off, be renewed, and put on.

quiz.q: What reason does Paul give for speaking truthfully to one another in verse 25?

options.text: Because we are all members of one another.
options.note: Truthful speech flows from our shared membership in the one body.

options.text: Because honesty always brings personal advantage.
options.note: The reason is our union as members of one another, not personal gain.

options.text: Because lying is merely impractical.
options.note: The deeper ground is that we belong to one another in the body.

options.text: Because the law demands it and nothing more.
options.note: Paul roots it in our new relationship as members of one body.

quiz.q: What does Paul say about anger in verses 26 and 27?

options.text: Do not sin in it, do not let it linger, and do not give the devil a foothold.
options.note: Anger must not turn to sin, fester overnight, or open a door to the devil.

options.text: All anger is sin and must be denied at once.
options.note: Paul says be angry yet do not sin, so the concern is anger that leads to sin and lingers.

options.text: Anger should be nursed and held onto as long as possible.
options.note: The command is not to let the sun set on it, the opposite of nursing it.

options.text: Anger gives no opportunity to the devil at all.
options.note: Paul warns that unresolved anger can give the devil a foothold.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by do not grieve the Holy Spirit in verse 30?

options.text: Sin pains the personal Spirit of God who indwells and seals believers.
options.note: The Spirit is a person who can be grieved, in whom we are sealed for redemption.

options.text: The Spirit is an impersonal force that cannot be affected.
options.note: Paul treats the Spirit as a person who can be grieved by sin.

options.text: We can lose the Spirit whenever we sin.
options.note: The verse stresses sealing for the day of redemption, not losing the Spirit.

options.text: The Spirit is only mildly concerned with our conduct.
options.note: The word grieve shows deep, personal concern, not mild interest.

quiz.q: On what does Paul ground the call to forgive one another in verse 32?

options.text: On the forgiveness God has shown us in Christ.
options.note: We forgive as in Christ God forgave us; grace received becomes grace given.

options.text: On the idea that the offense did not really matter.
options.note: Forgiveness releases a real wrong, grounded in God's forgiveness, not in minimizing.

options.text: On the hope that others will forgive us in return.
options.note: The ground is God's forgiveness in Christ, not a bargain with others.

options.text: On our own strength to overlook wrongs.
options.note: The motive is God's forgiving grace in Christ, not our own strength.

questions: Paul says the old way of thinking is futile and darkened. Where do you notice the leftover patterns of your old way of thinking still shaping how you live?
The new life is a great exchange: put off the old, put on the new. What is one specific thing God may be calling you to take off, and what would it look like to put on its opposite?
Verse 23 speaks of being renewed in the spirit of your mind. What are you feeding your mind, and how might God want to renew your thinking?
Paul says unresolved anger gives the devil a foothold. Is there anger you have let the sun go down on, and what would it take to deal with it before it festers?
Our words are to build others up and bring grace to those who listen. How would your speech change if that were its measure this week?
We forgive because God in Christ forgave us. Is there someone you are finding it hard to forgive, and how does remembering God's forgiveness of you help?

translationNotes.note: The BSB source used em dashes around the clause in keeping with the truth that is in Jesus; here they are rendered with commas to comply with the no-dash style, with no change to the wording.

This verse quotes Psalm 4:4, which the Greek Old Testament renders be angry and do not sin. Paul cites it to allow that anger may arise while forbidding it to become sin or to linger; the quotation is kept with straight double quotes.
Created to be like God renders a phrase meaning created after the likeness of God, echoing the image of God in Genesis, now restored in the new self through Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 4:17-32 reproduced as supplied. The em dashes in verse 21 were already converted to commas as supplied. The quotation of Psalm 4:4 in verse 26 is kept with straight double quotes. Inline tokens mark the seven supplied lexicon keys, each used once.
